ChatGPT said:

Yes — and your uploaded project is already pointed in the right direction.

You’re not really making a RetroArch “addon.” You’re making an external automation controller that sends inputs into RetroArch like a real controller. Your README already describes that exact architecture: shared interfaces, a deterministic scheduler, a Windows vgamepad backend, scripted playback, config, and logs.

Your current setup has four solid pieces:

  1. Action model
    ActionEvent stores an input like "a" or "right" plus how long to hold it.

  2. Input adapter contract
    InputBackend defines press, release, and flush, which is clean because you can later swap vgamepad for keyboard input or another backend.

  3. Frame-timed scheduler
    FrameScheduler runs actions on a fixed FPS loop, presses the button, flushes it, waits, releases it, and logs timing.

  4. Windows virtual controller backend
    VGamepadBackend maps "a", "b", "x", "y", and D-pad directions to an Xbox 360 virtual controller.

To run what you have now:

PowerShell
pip install vgamepad
python "g:/VSC projects/gameplay-automation-retroarch/src/scenarios/scripted_playback.py"

That matches your README quick start.

The current demo sequence presses:

Python
A → Right → B → Left → Y

That comes from _build_sequence() in scripted_playback.py.

My honest take: this is a good MVP, but it is only a scripted playback bot right now. It does not “see” the game yet. The next upgrade should be one of these:

Best next step: add config-driven playback so you can write routes in YAML instead of editing Python every time.

Example future default.yaml idea:

YAML
target_fps: 60
sequence:
  - action: a
    hold_seconds: 0.08
  - action: right
    hold_seconds: 0.30
  - action: a
    hold_seconds: 0.08

Then later you upgrade from “scripted bot” to “real bot” by adding a PerceptionBackend, which your interface file already planned for with sample().

So yes, Omar: you can build this. You’re already past the idea stage. You have the foundation for:

  • scripted TAS-style input playback,

  • repeatable farming routes,

  • menu automation,

  • simple “press this when image appears” bots,

  • eventually computer-vision bots using screenshots/OpenCV.

Just keep it to your own legal single-player ROMs/offline use. Don’t use this for online cheating.

ChatGPT said:

Got it — I see the updated upload.

It looks like the same staged RetroArch automation project again: README, core interfaces, scheduler, vgamepad backend, and scripted playback. The README confirms the project is meant to control RetroArch externally, with interfaces.py, scheduler.py, vgamepad_backend.py, scripted_playback.py, config defaults, and logs.

Your project is currently at Stage 1: scripted controller playback.

The clean upgrade path is:

Stage 2 — YAML playback
Make scripted_playback.py read button sequences from default.yaml instead of hardcoding them in _build_sequence(). Right now the sequence is fixed in Python: a, right, b, left, y.

Stage 3 — More buttons
Your VGamepadBackend currently supports only a, b, x, y, and D-pad directions. Next you should add Start, Select/Back, LB/RB, LT/RT, and analog sticks.

Stage 4 — Perception
You already planned for game-state sensing with PerceptionBackend.sample(), but it is only an interface right now. That is where screenshots, image detection, OCR, health bars, menus, or “wait until this screen appears” logic would go.

Best next move: I’d upgrade this into a config-driven RetroArch macro runner first. That gives you a useful tool before trying to build a smarter bot.


You said:

Ran terminal command: rg --files | rg -i "test|tests|pytest|unittest|spec" Completed with input: rg --files | rg -i "test|tests|pytest|unittest|spec" Ran terminal command: rg -n "yaml|default\.yaml|button_map|target_fps|scripted_playback|ActionEvent" src README.md config ## Context Map for: upgrade Stage 1 into a config-driven RetroArch macro runner (Stage 2 first, Stage 3-ready) ### Primary Files (directly modified) - scripted_playback.py — replace hardcoded sequence builder with YAML-driven sequence loading, plus runtime/log settings from config. - default.yaml — add a playback sequence section (ordered actions and hold times) to drive macros without code edits. ### Secondary Files (may need updates) - vgamepad_backend.py — align supported action names with config-defined actions; Stage 3 button expansion will happen here. - README.md — document YAML macro format and updated usage flow. ### Test Coverage - No test files detected in workspace. - Practical validation path: run scripted_playback.py and verify log output in the configured folder. ### Patterns to Follow - Event model pattern: interfaces.py - Timing/logging pattern: scheduler.py - Config source of truth: default.yaml ### Suggested Sequence 1. Extend default.yaml with a playback sequence list (action + hold_seconds). 2. Update scripted_playback.py to parse YAML, build ActionEvent objects, and consume runtime/logging config. 3. Keep existing defaults/fallbacks so current behavior still works if sequence data is missing. 4. Update README.md with the new config-driven macro format and run instructions. 5. Optional immediate follow-up: Stage 3 action expansion in vgamepad_backend.py (start/back, shoulders, triggers, sticks). Should I proceed with this plan, or would you like me to examine any of these files first?

ChatGPT said:

You’re at the point where the plan is good enough to execute. The key now is to keep Stage 2 small: YAML-driven playback only, not computer vision yet.

Yes — tell it to proceed. It already inspected the right files. Don’t let it over-scope into computer vision yet.

Your current project is clearly structured for external RetroArch control: interfaces, scheduler, vgamepad backend, scripted playback, config, and logs. The only real blocker is that _build_sequence() is still hardcoded in Python.

Paste this back into Copilot:

Proceed with Stage 2: config-driven YAML macro playback.

Keep scope tight. Do not add computer vision, OCR, or perception yet.

Implement these changes:

  1. Update config/default.yaml

    • Keep existing runtime, input, and logging sections.

    • Add a new playback.sequence list.

    • Each sequence item should support:

      • action

      • hold_seconds

    • Include a small demo sequence equivalent to the current hardcoded Python sequence.

  2. Update src/scenarios/scripted_playback.py

    • Load config/default.yaml.

    • Use runtime.target_fps for the scheduler.

    • Use logging.folder and logging.file_prefix for the log path.

    • Convert playback.sequence into ActionEvent objects.

    • Keep a fallback sequence matching the current hardcoded demo if the config has no sequence.

    • Add clear error messages for malformed config entries.

    • Keep direct script execution working from the scenarios folder.

  3. Add dependency handling

    • Use yaml.safe_load.

    • If PyYAML is missing, raise a helpful message: pip install pyyaml.

  4. Update README.md

    • Document the YAML macro format.

    • Show how to run the script.

    • Mention that logs are written to the configured logging folder.

    • Mention that supported actions currently depend on VGamepadBackend.

  5. Do not modify vgamepad_backend.py yet except if strictly necessary.

    • Stage 3 will add Start, Back/Select, LB/RB, LT/RT, and analog stick support.

Acceptance criteria:

  • Running python "g:/VSC projects/gameplay-automation-retroarch/src/scenarios/scripted_playback.py" reads the YAML sequence.

  • The script still works if the sequence is missing by using the fallback demo.

  • A JSONL playback log is created.

  • Unsupported actions fail clearly instead of silently doing nothing.

After Copilot patches it, test with:

PowerShell
pip install pyyaml vgamepad
python "g:/VSC projects/gameplay-automation-retroarch/src/scenarios/scripted_playback.py"

Verification target: RetroArch receives the same basic sequence, but now you can edit the macro from YAML instead of touching Python. Then Stage 3 is expanding the backend, because right now it only maps a, b, x, y, and D-pad directions.

I found your current config: it already has runtime, input, and logging, but no playback.sequence yet. I’m making the Stage 2 files match that exact layout instead of inventing a new structure.

Done — I made you a Stage 2 drop-in package so you can save Copilot credits.

Your current project already has the right skeleton: interfaces, scheduler, vgamepad backend, scripted playback, config, and logs. The main upgrade was replacing the hardcoded sequence in scripted_playback.py.

Download the full package here:

Individual files:

What I included:

  • YAML-driven macro playback.

  • Fallback demo sequence if playback.sequence is missing.

  • Friendly pip install pyyaml error if PyYAML is missing.

  • Runtime FPS from config.

  • Log folder and file prefix from config.

  • Clear malformed-config errors.

  • README instructions.

  • PowerShell apply script that backs up your current files before replacing them.

To apply manually, replace these in your project:

README.md
requirements.txt
config/default.yaml
src/scenarios/scripted_playback.py

Or after extracting the ZIP, run:

PowerShell
powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File .\scripts\apply_stage2.ps1

Then test:

PowerShell
cd "g:\VSC projects\gameplay-automation-retroarch"
pip install -r requirements.txt
python "g:/VSC projects/gameplay-automation-retroarch/src/scenarios/scripted_playback.py"

I also checked that the new scripted_playback.py compiles cleanly. Current supported actions are still only a, b, x, y, and D-pad directions because that’s what your backend maps right now.
